# /clip-repurposing-plan

Scans an episode transcript for moments strong enough to work as standalone short-form clips, and assigns each one a platform, caption hook, and cut-point timestamps.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

After an episode is recorded/transcribed, when the team needs to decide what to clip for social before handing the transcript to an editor - avoids the editor guessing which 15 seconds actually stand alone.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- The transcript, with timestamps if available
- Which platforms the show actively posts clips to (TikTok/Reels/Shorts, X/Twitter clips, LinkedIn, YouTube Shorts) - different platforms want different clip lengths and tones
- Number of clips needed (per episode or per week)
- Any topics that are off-limits to clip out of context (sensitive stories, legal risk, guest asked not to be clipped on a specific point)

## Workflow

1. Read the full transcript and mark every moment that works as a standalone unit - meaning a listener with zero episode context would understand and feel something (surprise, laughter, disagreement, a clear takeaway) within the first 3 seconds.
2. Reject "sounds fine in context but meaningless alone" moments - the single most common clipping mistake. Test each candidate by imagining it with no lead-in.
3. For each surviving moment, note the exact start/end timestamp (or nearest transcript marker) and write the one-line reason it works (the "hook").
4. Match each clip to the platform where its pacing fits: fast punchlines/hot takes to TikTok/Reels/Shorts (under 60s), a fuller argument or story to YouTube Shorts or LinkedIn (up to 90s), a sharp quotable line to X/Twitter as a short clip or text quote graphic.
5. Write a caption/hook line for each clip matched to that platform's norms (question-based hook for Reels, blunt statement for LinkedIn, quote-as-tweet for X).
6. Flag any strong moment that's risky to clip out of context (could misrepresent the guest, legal exposure, needs the surrounding nuance) so editors don't cut it thoughtlessly.
7. Rank the clips by likely performance (strongest hook first) so the editor prioritizes cutting the best ones if time is limited.

## Output format

```
CLIP PLAN - Episode [#]

RANKED CLIPS:
1. [MM:SS-MM:SS] - Platform: ... 
   Hook/why it works: ...
   Suggested caption: ...
2. ...

FLAGGED - risky to clip out of context:
- [MM:SS-MM:SS] - Reason: ...

Total clips recommended: [N]
```

## Do
- Test every candidate clip against the "zero context" rule before including it
- Match clip length and tone to the actual platform, not one-size-fits-all
- Rank by strength so editors know what to cut first under time pressure

## Don't
- Don't include a moment just because it's funny in full context if it falls flat without the setup
- Don't recommend clipping guest statements flagged as sensitive or off-limits
- Don't suggest more clips than the transcript actually supports - quality over quota

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Every clip passes the "makes sense with zero context" test
- [ ] Clips are matched to specific platforms, not generically listed
- [ ] Risky/off-limits moments are explicitly flagged, not silently included
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /clip-repurposing-plan or describe the task from the skill description.

# /cross-platform-distribution-checklist

Turns "we finished the episode" into a concrete, ordered checklist of exactly what gets posted where, in what format, and in what sequence - so nothing (or no platform) gets forgotten on a busy publish day.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

On or just before publish day, when an episode's audio, clips, and show notes are ready and the team needs a single checklist to work from instead of remembering platform-by-platform requirements from memory each week.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- Every platform the show actively publishes to (podcast host/RSS, YouTube full episode, YouTube Shorts/TikTok/Reels, X/Twitter, LinkedIn, newsletter, Discord/community)
- What assets already exist for this episode (full audio, video, clips, show notes, quote graphics) versus what's still pending
- Publish date/time and whether platforms should be staggered (e.g., audio drops first, clips trickle out over the following week) or launched simultaneously
- Any platform-specific account handles, hashtags, or series branding conventions already in use

## Workflow

1. List every asset the episode actually needs before assigning platforms - full audio file, episode art, show notes text, video cut (if applicable), 3-5 short clips, 1-2 quote graphics, newsletter blurb. Flag any asset marked "not ready yet" so it doesn't get lost.
2. Map each asset to its platform(s) with the exact format spec that platform needs (aspect ratio for video, character limit for the post copy, whether captions/subtitles are required).
3. Sequence the rollout, not just list it - typically full episode + show notes go live first, with clips staggered across the following days to extend the episode's reach rather than dumping everything the same hour.
4. Write the actual post copy (or a tight draft) for each platform post, matching that platform's norms - a LinkedIn post reads differently from an X post promoting the same clip.
5. Note cross-linking: every post should link back to the full episode (or to the next clip), so traffic funnels toward a listen, not just isolated engagement.
6. Build in a "who's responsible" and "status" column if multiple people handle different platforms, so the checklist doubles as a tracker, not just a to-do list.
7. Add a same-week reminder item to check platform analytics on the first clip/post and adjust the remaining rollout if something is under- or over-performing.

## Output format

```
DISTRIBUTION CHECKLIST - Episode [#] - Publish date: [date]

ASSETS STATUS:
- Full audio: [ready/pending]
- Show notes: [ready/pending]
- Video cut: [ready/pending/n/a]
- Clips (list, with status): ...
- Quote graphics: [ready/pending]

ROLLOUT SEQUENCE:
Day 0: [Platform] - [asset] - [post copy draft] - Owner: ... - Status: [ ]
Day 1-2: ...
Day 3-5: ...

Cross-link check: every post links to [full episode URL / next asset]
Mid-week check-in: review early performance, adjust remaining schedule
```

## Do
- List required assets before assigning platforms, so gaps surface early
- Stagger the rollout across several days rather than posting everything at once
- Write real draft post copy per platform, not a single copy-pasted caption everywhere

## Don't
- Don't treat every platform post as identical copy with a different platform name swapped in
- Don't publish clips without a link back to the full episode
- Don't mark an asset "ready" if it's still pending - flag gaps honestly so the team can react

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Every platform has its own tailored post copy, not one recycled caption
- [ ] Rollout is sequenced over time, not a same-day dump
- [ ] Asset status (ready/pending) is honestly reflected, not assumed complete
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /cross-platform-distribution-checklist or describe the task from the skill description.

# /episode-brief-generator

Turns a topic, guest, or news hook into a structured pre-recording brief the host can read in five minutes before hitting record.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

The night before or morning of a recording session, when the host has a topic or guest booked but hasn't yet converted it into something usable at the mic - especially for solo shows or interview shows where "winging it" has burned an episode before.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- Episode topic or guest name, and why this episode is happening now (news hook, launch, recurring segment)
- Format: solo monologue, co-host banter, or interview
- Target runtime (drives how many talking points/questions you can fit)
- Anything the host has already said publicly on this topic (to avoid repeating old takes) or must avoid saying (legal, past falling-out, NDA topic)

## Workflow

1. State the episode's single-sentence "point" - the one idea a listener should be able to repeat to a friend afterward. Reject vague topics until this sentence exists.
2. Build a cold open hook: one line or story the host can say in the first 30 seconds that is not "welcome back to the show."
3. For interviews, draft 8-12 questions ordered by narrative arc (origin story → conflict/turning point → present state → forward-looking/advice), not just a topic checklist. Mark 2-3 as "if time allows" so the host knows what to cut first.
4. For solo/co-host formats, draft 4-6 talking points as beats with a one-line thesis each, not just bullet keywords - the host should be able to speak from the beat alone.
5. Flag risk items: anything litigious, a past guest conflict, a claim that needs a source, or a segment that has run long in prior episodes.
6. Add a "listener callback" note if this episode should reference a past episode, running joke, or ongoing storyline the audience follows.
7. Close with a landing line - how the episode should wrap (CTA, teaser for next episode, or thesis restate) so it doesn't trail off.

## Output format

```
EPISODE BRIEF - [Episode # / working title]
One-sentence point: ...
Cold open hook: ...

[If interview] Question order:
1. ...
...
(if time allows: ...)

[If solo/co-host] Talking point beats:
1. Beat - thesis
...

Callback to prior episode: ... (or "none")
Risk flags: ... (or "none")
Landing line: ...
```

## Do
- Write questions/beats as sentences the host could say out loud, not keyword fragments
- Order interview questions by story arc, not by the order topics occurred to you
- Keep the whole brief to one page - it's read standing up, minutes before recording

## Don't
- Don't write more than 12 interview questions - hosts skim past long lists mid-recording
- Don't bury the risk flags at the end in prose; they must be scannable in isolation
- Don't invent guest biographical facts - if you don't have verified guest background, say so and ask for it rather than guessing

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] One-sentence point is genuinely specific to this episode, not a restated topic
- [ ] Question/beat order follows a narrative arc, not a random list
- [ ] Risk flags section is present even when empty ("none")
```

# /episode-performance-review

Turns raw episode analytics (downloads, completion rate, drop-off points) into a plain-English explanation of performance versus the show's own baseline, with specific next-episode adjustments - not a generic "engagement was good" summary.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

A few days to a week after an episode publishes, when enough data has accumulated to compare it against the show's typical numbers - particularly useful when an episode clearly over- or under-performed and the team wants a real explanation rather than a shrug.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- This episode's stats: downloads/listens (at 7-day and 30-day if available), completion/retention rate, and drop-off timestamp data if the platform provides it
- The show's baseline/typical numbers for the same metrics, so this episode has something real to compare against (never invent a baseline - ask if unknown)
- Episode context: format (solo/interview), topic, guest profile, publish day/time, any promotion push that was different from usual
- What the team actually wants to learn (raw reach, retention/completion, or conversion into subscribers/reviews)

## Workflow

1. Compare this episode's numbers to the show's actual baseline, not to an arbitrary "good" number - state the percentage above/below normal explicitly.
2. If drop-off data exists, identify the specific timestamp(s) where listeners left in unusual numbers and cross-reference that point against the transcript/rundown - a slow intro, a tangent, or an ad placement is often the real cause.
3. Separate variables that affected reach (title, thumbnail, promotion, publish timing, guest's own audience/sharing) from variables that affected retention (pacing, content quality, episode length) - these are different problems with different fixes.
4. If the episode outperformed, identify the likely specific cause (guest draw, topic timeliness, a clip that went viral) so it can be deliberately repeated rather than attributed vaguely to "good episode."

# /guest-outreach-email

Drafts a guest invitation email (and a shorter follow-up) that proves the host actually knows the guest's work, rather than a mail-merge pitch.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

When the producer has identified a specific person to book and needs an email that will actually get a reply, versus a generic "love your work, come on my show" template that experienced guests ignore.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- Guest name and the specific piece of their work (book, project, tweet, talk, recent news) prompting this invite
- Show name, format, typical episode length, and audience size/niche (guests weigh this before saying yes)
- Whether this is cold outreach, warm (mutual connection/prior interaction), or a returning guest
- Proposed recording logistics: remote/in-person, rough date window, and whether there's compensation or it's unpaid

## Workflow

1. Open with the specific hook - name the exact piece of work, quote, or moment that made you want this guest, not "I've been a fan of your work." Generic praise is the #1 reason these emails get ignored.
2. State in one sentence what the episode would actually be about with them specifically - not "come talk about your career," but the angle only this guest could deliver.
3. Give concrete logistics up front (format, length, remote or in-person, rough timeframe) so the guest isn't left guessing about commitment level before deciding.
4. Include one sentence of social proof only if genuinely relevant (notable past guests, audience size, download numbers) - skip it if the show is new; don't inflate numbers.
5. Make the ask low-friction: propose 2-3 concrete date/time windows or a scheduling link, not an open-ended "let me know when works."
6. Close with an easy out ("no worries if the timing's not right") - guests are more likely to reply to low-pressure asks.
7. Draft a shorter (3-4 sentence) polite follow-up to send if there's no response after 5-7 business days.

## Output format

```
SUBJECT: ...

INITIAL EMAIL:
Hi [Name],

[Specific hook - their work, named]
[Episode angle specific to them]
[Format/logistics: length, remote/in-person, timeframe]
[Optional: one line social proof]
[Ask: 2-3 time windows or scheduling link]
[Easy out line]

Best,
[Host name]

---
FOLLOW-UP (send after 5-7 business days if no reply):
...
```

## Do
- Reference something real and specific about the guest's actual work - this is the entire difference between a reply and a delete
- Keep the initial email under 150 words - busy guests skim
- Give concrete date options rather than open-ended scheduling asks

## Don't
- Don't use "I've been a huge fan" or "long-time listener" style flattery with no specifics behind it
- Don't bury the ask - logistics and the ask should be visible without scrolling
- Don't inflate audience numbers or past guest names to sound more impressive than the show actually is

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Hook references something concrete and specific to this guest, not generic praise
- [ ] Logistics (length, format, timeframe) are stated plainly, not implied
- [ ] Follow-up email is shorter and lower-pressure than the initial ask
```

# /guest-prep-packet

Produces the outward-facing packet sent to a confirmed guest ahead of recording: logistics, mic/tech setup, and a topic preview - built to reduce guest nerves and no-shows, not the host's internal question list.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

After a guest confirms and before the recording date, especially for first-time podcast guests who don't know what to expect technically or conversationally - reduces day-of tech scrambles and guest anxiety about "what will I be asked."

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- Recording date/time, timezone, platform (Zoom/Riverside/StreamYard/in-person), and expected duration
- Whether this is the guest's first time on a podcast (changes how much tech hand-holding is needed) or a returning/experienced guest
- Broad topic areas planned (not the full host question list - guests generally shouldn't get the exact script, to keep answers natural)
- Any release/consent form, usage rights, or compensation details that need to be communicated

## Workflow

1. Confirm logistics first and unambiguously - exact date, start time with timezone spelled out, platform/link, and expected total time commitment including any pre-call buffer.
2. Give plain-language technical setup guidance suited to the guest's experience level: quiet room, headphones (not earbuds, if that's the show's preference), stable internet, camera framing - skip this section or shorten it for experienced guest podcasters.
3. Share a topic preview at the "themes we'll explore" level, not verbatim questions - this keeps answers conversational rather than rehearsed, while still letting the guest prepare mentally and avoid being blindsided.
4. Note anything explicitly off-limits or being asked NOT to prep (e.g., "no need to prepare stats, we'll keep this conversational") so guests don't over-prepare into a stiff, scripted-sounding interview.
5. Include what happens after recording: rough publish date, whether they'll get an advance listen/review window, and how they can share/promote the episode once live.
6. State clearly who to contact and how for last-minute tech issues or reschedule needs, with a specific channel (not "reach out if there's a problem").
7. Keep tone warm and low-pressure throughout - this document is partly a hospitality artifact, not just a logistics memo.

## Output format

```
GUEST PREP PACKET - [Show name]
Hi [Guest name], excited to have you on!

LOGISTICS:
Date/time (with timezone): ...
Platform/link: ...
Expected duration: ...

TECH SETUP:
...

WHAT WE'LL TALK ABOUT:
[Theme-level preview, not scripted questions]
No need to prepare: ...

AFTER RECORDING:
Publish date (approx): ...
Review/advance listen: ...
How to share once live: ...

QUESTIONS OR LAST-MINUTE CHANGES:
Contact: [name/channel]
```

## Do
- Give themes, not the verbatim question list - keeps the actual conversation natural
- Calibrate tech detail to the guest's experience level rather than a one-size-fits-all packet
- State timezone explicitly, every time - the most common no-show cause is timezone confusion

## Don't
- Don't send the host's full internal question script - that produces flat, over-rehearsed answers
- Don't skip the "what happens after" section - guests care about promo timing and control over their appearance
- Don't bury the reschedule/contact info at the bottom in small print

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Timezone is stated explicitly alongside the time
- [ ] Topic preview is theme-level, not a verbatim question list
- [ ] Tone is warm/welcoming, not a dry logistics memo
```

# /listener-qa-batch-processor

Takes a raw dump of listener questions (from email, DMs, a form, or comments) and turns it into an ordered, deduplicated segment script with suggested answer angles.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Before recording a listener mailbag/Q&A/AMA segment, when questions have piled up across multiple channels and the host doesn't have time to manually sort through fifty submissions to find the twelve worth answering on air.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- The raw list of listener questions (paste as-is; include source/username if available for on-air credit)
- How many questions the segment needs (time-boxed by segment length)
- Any recurring questions already answered in past episodes (to avoid repeating, or to flag as "worth revisiting")
- Sensitive-topic policy - are there question types (medical, legal, personal-life-prying) the host won't answer on air

## Workflow

1. Read every submitted question first, then group near-duplicates and variations-on-a-theme into single clusters - count how many people asked essentially the same thing, since frequency signals what the audience actually wants addressed.
2. Rank clusters by a mix of frequency and how interesting/answerable they are on air - a question ten people asked beats a unique but low-value one, but a single fascinating question can outrank a common shallow one.
3. Cut anything that's really a customer-service request, a pitch, or spam disguised as a question - route those aside, don't put them in the segment.
4. Flag any question touching a sensitive or previously-off-limits topic so the host can decide deliberately whether to answer, redirect, or skip rather than encountering it live.
5. For each selected question, write a one-line "answer angle" - not the full answer, but the core point the host should hit, so they're not answering cold.
6. Order final segment questions from lighter/quicker to more substantial, saving one strong closing question for the end of the segment.
7. Note attribution (name/handle) for each question included, respecting anonymity if the submission requested it.

## Output format

```
Q&A SEGMENT - [Episode/date]
Total questions received: [N] | Selected for segment: [N]

SELECTED QUESTIONS (in segment order):
1. "[Question]" - asked by [N people / handle]
   Answer angle: ...
2. ...

FLAGGED - sensitive, needs host judgment call:
- "[Question]" - Reason: ...

SET ASIDE - not genuine questions (spam/pitch/support request):
- [count] items, examples: ...
```

## Do
- Cluster duplicate/near-duplicate questions before ranking - raw frequency is a real signal
- Give a one-line angle per question, not a full scripted answer - the host should still sound spontaneous
- Preserve anonymity when a submitter requested it

## Don't
- Don't include spam, pitches, or support tickets disguised as questions in the segment
- Don't silently skip sensitive questions - flag them for an explicit host decision
- Don't write full scripted answers that would make the segment sound canned

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Duplicate/near-duplicate questions are merged with a frequency count, not listed separately
- [ ] Sensitive questions are flagged for a decision, not auto-included or auto-excluded
- [ ] Segment count matches the time-box the user specified
```

# /listener-qa-batch-processor

Takes a raw dump of listener questions (from email, DMs, a form, or comments) and turns it into an ordered, deduplicated segment script with suggested answer angles.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Before recording a listener mailbag/Q&A/AMA segment, when questions have piled up across multiple channels and the host doesn't have time to manually sort through fifty submissions to find the twelve worth answering on air.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- The raw list of listener questions (paste as-is; include source/username if available for on-air credit)
- How many questions the segment needs (time-boxed by segment length)
- Any recurring questions already answered in past episodes (to avoid repeating, or to flag as "worth revisiting")
- Sensitive-topic policy - are there question types (medical, legal, personal-life-prying) the host won't answer on air

## Workflow

1. Read every submitted question first, then group near-duplicates and variations-on-a-theme into single clusters - count how many people asked essentially the same thing, since frequency signals what the audience actually wants addressed.
2. Rank clusters by a mix of frequency and how interesting/answerable they are on air - a question ten people asked beats a unique but low-value one, but a single fascinating question can outrank a common shallow one.
3. Cut anything that's really a customer-service request, a pitch, or spam disguised as a question - route those aside, don't put them in the segment.
4. Flag any question touching a sensitive or previously-off-limits topic so the host can decide deliberately whether to answer, redirect, or skip rather than encountering it live.
5. For each selected question, write a one-line "answer angle" - not the full answer, but the core point the host should hit, so they're not answering cold.
6. Order final segment questions from lighter/quicker to more substantial, saving one strong closing question for the end of the segment.
7. Note attribution (name/handle) for each question included, respecting anonymity if the submission requested it.

## Output format

```
Q&A SEGMENT - [Episode/date]
Total questions received: [N] | Selected for segment: [N]

SELECTED QUESTIONS (in segment order):
1. "[Question]" - asked by [N people / handle]
   Answer angle: ...
2. ...

FLAGGED - sensitive, needs host judgment call:
- "[Question]" - Reason: ...

SET ASIDE - not genuine questions (spam/pitch/support request):
- [count] items, examples: ...
```

## Do
- Cluster duplicate/near-duplicate questions before ranking - raw frequency is a real signal
- Give a one-line angle per question, not a full scripted answer - the host should still sound spontaneous
- Preserve anonymity when a submitter requested it

## Don't
- Don't include spam, pitches, or support tickets disguised as questions in the segment
- Don't silently skip sensitive questions - flag them for an explicit host decision
- Don't write full scripted answers that would make the segment sound canned

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Duplicate/near-duplicate questions are merged with a frequency count, not listed separately
- [ ] Sensitive questions are flagged for a decision, not auto-included or auto-excluded
- [ ] Segment count matches the time-box the user specified

# /listener-survey-designer

Builds a short, decision-driven listener survey - the question set, order, and distribution plan - anchored to one real decision the show is weighing, rather than a generic "how are we doing" poll nobody finishes.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

When the show is weighing a real decision - changing episode length, adding a co-host, dropping a segment, testing a new format - and wants actual listener input rather than guessing, or before an annual "state of the show" check-in.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- The specific decision or open question driving this survey (if there isn't one yet, that's the first thing to nail down - a survey with no decision behind it collects data nobody acts on)
- Where the survey will be distributed (newsletter, show outro CTA, social, Discord/community) - affects expected response volume and appropriate length
- Anything already known/assumed about listener preferences that the survey should test rather than re-ask
- Incentive, if any, being offered for completion (shoutout, giveaway, early access)

## Workflow

1. Force clarity on the decision first - write the one-sentence question the show actually needs answered (e.g., "should we move from 60 to 90 minute episodes?"). If none exists, say so and propose one based on what the user described.
2. Design every survey question to serve that decision - cut any question that's merely "nice to know" but wouldn't change what the show does with the answer.
3. Keep total length under 8 questions for a general audience survey - completion rates fall sharply past that, and the decision-relevant questions matter more than breadth.
4. Order questions from easy/low-effort (multiple choice, rating scale) to any open-text question, and put open-text last - early open-text questions cause higher abandonment.
5. Include one segmenting question near the top (e.g., how long they've listened, how they usually listen) only if it will actually be used to cross-tab answers - otherwise cut it to save the respondent's time.
6. Write the actual distribution copy (the ask, in the show's voice) for each channel it'll be posted to, since a Discord post and a newsletter blurb need different framing/length.
7. Note explicitly how the results will be used and shared back with the audience if promised - following up publicly on survey results builds trust for the next one.

## Output format

```
LISTENER SURVEY - Decision this serves: [one sentence]

QUESTIONS (in order):
1. [Question] - Type: [multiple choice/scale/open text] - Why this question matters to the decision: ...
2. ...
(max 8 questions)

DISTRIBUTION COPY:
[Channel 1, e.g. show outro]: ...
[Channel 2, e.g. newsletter]: ...
[Channel 3, e.g. Discord/social]: ...

Incentive (if any): ...
Plan for sharing results back with listeners: ...
```

## Do
- Anchor every question to the one real decision - cut anything merely interesting but not actionable
- Keep the survey short enough to actually get finished
- Write distribution copy per channel rather than one generic blurb

## Don't
- Don't build a survey with no decision behind it - that produces data nobody uses
- Don't front-load open-text questions before the quick multiple-choice ones
- Don't promise to share results back with the audience and then have no plan to actually do it

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Every question ties back to the stated decision - none are "just curious" filler
- [ ] Question count is 8 or fewer for a general-audience survey
- [ ] Distribution copy is written per channel, not copy-pasted identically everywhere
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /listener-survey-designer or describe the task from the skill description.

# /pre-record-tech-checklist

Produces a technical go/no-go checklist matched to the show's specific recording setup (solo/remote-guest/in-studio), designed to catch the actual failure points that ruin recordings - not a generic "check your mic" list.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Before any recording session, especially ones with remote guests, new equipment, or a setup change (new mic, new platform, new location) where something is more likely to go wrong than on a routine solo recording.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- Recording setup: solo in one location, co-hosts together, or remote guest(s) joining via a call platform
- Recording software/platform in use (local double-ender, Riverside/Squadcast-style remote recording, Zoom, in-person multi-track)
- Backup practices already in place (local recording as backup to cloud, spare mic, secondary device) versus what's missing
- Anything unusual about this specific session (new equipment being tested for the first time, guest with known past connectivity issues, live/no-edit recording)

## Workflow

1. Separate the checklist by role - items only the host needs to check (local mic, room noise, lighting) versus items relevant to remote guests (their mic/headphones, their internet stability, their local backup recording) - a generic combined list gets skipped.
2. Put "local backup recording is running" at or near the top for any remote setup - the single most common irrecoverable failure is losing a guest's audio to a dropped connection with no local backup, and this is preventable but frequently forgotten.
3. Include a live audio level check with the actual person talking (not silence) immediately before recording starts - clipping and too-quiet levels are only caught by someone actually speaking into the mic at monitored volume.
4. For remote guests, add a "test their end explicitly" step - ask the guest to say a test sentence and confirm both the host hears them clearly and the guest can hear playback, rather than assuming a video call connecting means audio quality is fine.
5. If new equipment or software is being used for the first time this session, add an explicit dry-run step days before, not minutes before, recording.
6. Include a same-page confirmation on recording start/stop responsibility (who presses record, who confirms both local and remote tracks are capturing) to avoid the "I thought you were recording" failure.
7. Add a short post-check immediately after recording stops - confirm the file exists, has audio, and is the expected rough length - before the guest disconnects, so a lost recording can still be salvaged live.

## Output format

```
PRE-RECORD TECH CHECKLIST - [Session type: solo/co-host/remote guest]

HOST-SIDE:
[ ] Mic connected and levels checked (live, with speech)
[ ] Room noise check (fans, notifications off)
[ ] Local backup recording running
[ ] Lighting/camera check (if video)

REMOTE GUEST-SIDE (if applicable):
[ ] Guest audio tested live (test sentence, not silence)
[ ] Guest confirmed hearing playback clearly
[ ] Guest's local backup recording running (if platform requires it)
[ ] Internet stability check / backup contact method agreed

RECORDING CONFIRMATION:
[ ] Who presses record: ...
[ ] Who confirms both tracks capturing: ...

IMMEDIATELY AFTER STOPPING:
[ ] File exists, has audio, roughly expected length - confirmed before guest disconnects

New equipment/software this session: [dry-run completed? y/n]
```

## Do
- Split checklist items by who is responsible (host vs. remote guest) rather than one undifferentiated list
- Require a live speech test for audio levels, not a silent check
- Confirm the recording file is valid before the guest disconnects, while it's still fixable

## Don't
- Don't assume a connected video call means usable audio quality without an explicit test
- Don't skip the dry-run step when new equipment or software is being used for the first time
- Don't let the session end without confirming the recording actually captured

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Checklist is split by role (host vs. remote guest), not generic
- [ ] Local backup recording is explicitly checked for any remote setup
- [ ] Post-recording file verification happens before the guest disconnects
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /pre-record-tech-checklist or describe the task from the skill description.

# /season-arc-planner

Takes a season's worth of topic ideas, booked guests, and a target episode count, and sequences them into an arc with intentional pacing - not just a list in the order ideas came up.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

Before a new season or major batch of episodes starts recording, when the host has a pile of topic ideas and maybe a few guests booked but no sequencing logic yet - prevents two similar episodes airing back-to-back or the season front-loading all the strong material.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- List of topic ideas and/or booked guests for the season, with rough notes on each
- Target number of episodes and release cadence (weekly, biweekly)
- Season theme or throughline if one already exists (or note that the AI should propose one from the material)
- Any fixed-date constraints (a guest only available a specific week, a launch tied to a real-world event)

## Workflow

1. Group the raw topic/guest list into 2-4 natural clusters (e.g., by theme, by difficulty/depth, by tone) - this reveals whether the season has enough range or is secretly five variations of the same episode.
2. Identify or propose the season's throughline: the sentence that describes what changes or builds across the season, even for interview shows without a strict narrative (e.g., "each episode goes one layer deeper into X").
3. Place the strongest single episode (best guest, most compelling topic) at position 2 or 3, not episode 1 - episode 1 needs to set context/tone, and burning the best material immediately gives listeners no reason to stay subscribed.
4. Alternate tone and format across the sequence (e.g., don't stack three heavy/serious episodes back to back, don't put two interview episodes with overlapping subject matter next to each other).
5. Build in one lighter or "palate cleanser" episode roughly every 4-5 episodes if the season is topic-heavy, to prevent listener fatigue.
6. Reserve the final 1-2 episode slots for a season-closer that explicitly answers or revisits the throughline, plus a teaser/setup for what's next.
7. Flag any fixed-date guest/topic constraints as hard anchors first, then sequence the flexible material around them.

## Output format

```
SEASON PLAN - [Season name/number]
Throughline: ...
Episode count: [N] | Cadence: ...

EPISODE SEQUENCE:
Ep 1 - [Topic/guest] - Role in arc: (season opener/context-setter)
Ep 2 - [Topic/guest] - Role in arc: ...
...
Ep [N-1/N] - [Topic/guest] - Role in arc: (closer/throughline payoff)

Tone/pacing notes: ...
Hard-date constraints: ...
Gaps to fill (if fewer ideas than episode slots): ...
```

## Do
- Cluster and check for range before sequencing - a season of look-alike episodes is the most common failure mode
- Deliberately place the strongest material a few episodes in, not first
- Call out gaps explicitly if there aren't enough ideas to fill the target episode count

## Don't
- Don't sequence episodes in the order ideas happened to be listed
- Don't stack similar tones/formats back-to-back without flagging it
- Don't force a throughline onto material that doesn't have one - say so and propose a loose organizing theme instead

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Sequence shows deliberate pacing logic, not source-list order
- [ ] Strongest material is not placed at episode 1
- [ ] Any gaps or fixed-date constraints are called out explicitly
```

2. Identify or propose the season's throughline: the sentence that describes what changes or builds across the season, even for interview shows without a strict narrative (e.g., "each episode goes one layer deeper into X").
3. Place the strongest single episode (best guest, most compelling topic) at position 2 or 3, not episode 1 - episode 1 needs to set context/tone, and burning the best material immediately gives listeners no reason to stay subscribed.

5. Build in one lighter or "palate cleanser" episode roughly every 4-5 episodes if the season is topic-heavy, to prevent listener fatigue.
6. Reserve the final 1-2 episode slots for a season-closer that explicitly answers or revisits the throughline, plus a teaser/setup for what's next.
7. Flag any fixed-date guest/topic constraints as hard anchors first, then sequence the flexible material around them.

Episode count: [N] | Cadence: ...

Gaps to fill (if fewer ideas than episode slots): ...

- Call out gaps explicitly if there aren't enough ideas to fill the target episode count

Reads a full episode transcript and produces the show notes page: summary, timestamped chapters, pull quotes, and mentioned resources - ready to paste into a podcast host or blog.

## Works with

Right after an episode is recorded and transcribed (auto-transcript or manual), when the producer needs the show notes published alongside the audio and doesn't want to re-listen to the whole episode to build timestamps by hand.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- The transcript text (with timestamps if available; if not, note that chapter markers will be approximate/ordinal instead of time-coded)
- Episode number, title, and guest name(s) if any
- Any products, books, tools, or links mentioned that need real URLs (flag these - don't invent URLs)
- Preferred show notes length: short (for social/podcast app description) vs. long (for a blog post)

## Workflow

1. Read the full transcript first before drafting anything - chapter breaks should follow actual topic shifts in the conversation, not even time intervals.
2. Write a 2-3 sentence summary that would make sense to someone who has never heard of the show, avoiding insider references.
3. Build timestamped chapters at natural topic transitions (aim for 5-10 chapters on a typical episode) - label each with what's discussed, not a vague word like "Discussion."
4. Pull 2-4 direct quotes verbatim from the transcript that are quotable outside the episode's context (works as a standalone tweet/graphic). Attribute correctly to the speaker who said it.

Guest(s): ...

SHORT DESCRIPTION (podcast app, ~2 sentences):
...

SUMMARY (blog intro, 2-3 sentences):
...

CHAPTERS:
[00:00] Intro
[MM:SS] Topic label
...

KEY QUOTES:
"..." - Speaker Name
...

MENTIONED IN THIS EPISODE:
- Item - [link needed] or [URL if provided]
...

CTA / Sign-off:
...
```

- Quote verbatim - clean up only filler words (um, uh), never change what was actually said
- Mark missing links explicitly as "[link needed]" rather than guessing a URL
- Match chapter count to actual topic changes, not a fixed formula

- Don't fabricate timestamps if the transcript has none - use ordinal markers ("Segment 1") instead and say so
- Don't paraphrase quotes and present them as direct quotes
- Don't pad the mentioned-resources list with things not actually named in the transcript

- [ ] Every quote is checked against the transcript for exact wording
- [ ] Chapters reflect real topic shifts, not mechanical time slicing
- [ ] No invented URLs - all unverified links marked "[link needed]"

# /sponsor-read-script

Drafts a sponsor read the host can perform live or record, sounding like the host talking, not like copied ad copy, while hitting every required disclosure and claim the sponsor mandates.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

When a sponsorship deal is signed and the producer needs a script the host can read naturally, especially for hosts who sound stiff reading brand-supplied copy verbatim.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- Sponsor name, product, and the specific offer/promo code/link for this episode
- Required legal or brand talking points the sponsor mandates verbatim (get exact wording - don't paraphrase claims like health, financial, or safety benefits)
- Ad placement (pre-roll = short/high-energy, mid-roll = longer/can include a personal anecdote, post-roll = brief/low-pressure)
- Host's usual speaking style - casual, deadpan, enthusiastic - so the read matches their other episodes

## Safety

- Propose before acting: show the draft script and flag any claim that needs sponsor legal sign-off; wait for explicit confirmation before treating it as final/air-ready.
- Never invent or soften a mandated legal disclosure - if the sponsor requires exact wording (e.g., financial risk disclosures, health claim disclaimers), reproduce it exactly and mark it as non-negotiable in the script.

## Workflow

1. Open with a natural bridge from the episode content into the ad, not a hard "and now a word from our sponsor" unless that's the show's established style.
2. Write the read in first person as the host, including one authentic-sounding personal detail or opinion (real use case, honest caveat) - reads that sound like copy-paste ad text get skipped by listeners.
3. Insert all sponsor-mandated claims verbatim and flag them inline as `[MANDATORY - do not paraphrase]` so the host knows not to ad-lib that line.
4. Place the offer/promo code near the end, repeated once, spoken the way a human would say it aloud (spell out tricky codes phonetically).
5. Match length to placement: pre-roll ~15-20 seconds read time, mid-roll 45-75 seconds, post-roll ~15 seconds.
6. Add a one-line CTA restating exactly where to go/what code to use, as the very last sentence before returning to the show.
7. Include a short "ad-lib alternate" version (bullet points instead of full script) for hosts who prefer to read notes and speak naturally rather than a fixed script.

## Output format

```
SPONSOR READ - [Brand] - [Placement: pre/mid/post-roll]
Estimated read time: ...

FULL SCRIPT:
[Bridge line]
[Host-voice body copy, mandatory claims marked inline]
[Offer + code, phonetic if needed]
[CTA / return-to-show line]

AD-LIB ALTERNATE (bullet points):
- ...
- ...

Mandatory claims requiring sponsor sign-off before airing:
- ...
```

## Do
- Keep the host's real voice and speech patterns - read it aloud mentally before finalizing
- Isolate mandatory/legal language clearly so it can't accidentally be cut in editing
- Offer the bullet-point ad-lib version alongside the full script

## Don't
- Don't soften, cut, or paraphrase a sponsor's mandatory legal/claim language
- Don't write generic ad copy that could apply to any host on any show
- Don't mark anything as "ready to air" without an explicit user go-ahead - sponsor claims often need legal review first

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Approval gate present before treating the script as final/air-ready
- [ ] Mandatory sponsor language is verbatim and clearly flagged, not paraphrased
- [ ] Read length matches the stated placement (pre/mid/post-roll)
```

# /sponsorship-pitch-deck-outline

Produces the outline, slide-by-slide content, and speaker notes for a deck used to pitch a specific brand on sponsoring the show - distinct from a status report, this is a sales document meant to close a deal.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

When the show is approaching (or has been approached by) a potential sponsor and needs a pitch deck outline to send or present - not a generic media kit, but a pitch tailored to why this brand specifically fits this audience.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- Show's actual audience numbers: downloads/listens per episode, demographic/geographic breakdown, growth trend if available (never invent numbers - ask for real data or mark placeholders clearly)
- The specific brand being pitched and why their product/service is a plausible fit for this audience
- Available ad inventory and formats (pre/mid/post-roll, dedicated episode, social bundle) and any pricing already decided
- Past sponsor results if any (retention rate, redemption/conversion data from prior codes) to use as proof

## Workflow

1. Open the deck with the audience-fit thesis on slide 1 or 2 - a single sentence connecting this specific brand's customer to this specific show's listener, not a generic "engaged audience" claim.
2. Build an audience snapshot slide using only real, provided numbers - mark any unavailable metric as `[data needed]` rather than estimating or rounding favorably.
3. Include a "why this show" slide differentiating from generic podcast ad inventory - host's authentic voice, topical relevance to the sponsor's category, or a niche the brand can't reach through mass-market ads.
4. Lay out inventory options as a clear menu (placement type, length, frequency, price) so the sponsor can see choices rather than one take-it-or-leave-it package.
5. If past sponsor performance data exists, add a proof slide with real redemption/click numbers - skip this slide entirely if no real data exists rather than filling it with vague claims.
6. Add a "what a partnership could look like" slide sketching a concrete 3-month or season-long scenario specific to this brand's product, not a generic package description.
7. Close with a clear single next step (a call, a trial episode, a signed IO) rather than an open-ended "let's talk."

## Output format

```
SPONSORSHIP PITCH DECK OUTLINE - Pitching: [Brand]

Slide 1 - Title / hook: ...
Slide 2 - Audience-fit thesis: ...
Slide 3 - Audience snapshot: [metrics, marking any [data needed]]
Slide 4 - Why this show (differentiation): ...
Slide 5 - Inventory menu: [placement | length | frequency | price]
Slide 6 - Proof (past sponsor results) - [include only if real data provided]
Slide 7 - Proposed partnership scenario for [Brand] specifically: ...
Slide 8 - Next step / CTA: ...

Speaker notes for slides 2, 4, and 7 (the persuasion slides): ...
```

## Do
- Anchor the whole pitch in why this brand fits this audience, stated in concrete terms, not generic sponsorship boilerplate
- Mark any missing metric as `[data needed]` instead of estimating
- Give a menu of inventory options rather than a single fixed package

## Don't
- Don't invent download numbers, demographics, or past redemption rates
- Don't include a proof-of-results slide if there's no real data to put on it
- Don't end without one specific, actionable next step

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] All audience/performance numbers are either real or explicitly marked `[data needed]`
- [ ] Audience-fit thesis names the specific brand, not a generic sponsor
- [ ] Deck ends with one concrete next step, not an open-ended close
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /sponsorship-pitch-deck-outline or describe the task from the skill description.
